situation.athlete_state: ATL 72, CTL 58, tomorrow threshold workout

conversation_turns.content: Should I run easy today or take off?

user_message: Should I run easy today or take off?

expected_coaching_truths.description: Today's choice should protect tomorrow's quality workout.

baseline_answer: Run 30-40 minutes very easy only if it makes you feel better; otherwise take the day off so tomorrow's threshold session is sharper.
baseline_comparison_rubric: StrideIQ must use load and plan context
StrideIQ should exceed baseline by citing tomorrow's workout
passing_answer: Default: 30-40 minutes easy or off, not a workout. ATL 72 is sitting above CTL 58 and tomorrow is the threshold session, so today only exists to make tomorrow better.
failing_answer: Listen to your body. If you feel good, push a little because consistency matters.

situation.athlete_state: three hard days in five, HRV down, athlete insists they feel fine
situation.context: workout urge after a strong week

conversation_turns.content: I feel fine after the workout yesterday. Should I add another hard run today?

user_message: I feel fine after the workout yesterday. Should I add another hard run today?

expected_coaching_truths.description: Do not green-light another hard day after recent density.

baseline_answer: No hard run today. Feeling good is useful data, but the recent hard-day density means easy or off is the right coaching call.
baseline_comparison_rubric: StrideIQ should give a replacement session

retrieved_evidence.content: three hard days in five days

passing_answer: No hard run today. You have three hard days in five and HRV down, so feeling fine does not override the pattern; run easy recovery or skip and keep the next quality day intact.
failing_answer: If you feel fine, send it with a light tempo today.

situation.athlete_state: no active plan, moderate load, last quality session 6 days ago

conversation_turns.content: I do not have a plan right now. What should I do today?

user_message: I do not have a plan right now. What should I do today?
required_context: last quality touch was six days ago

expected_coaching_truths.description: Offer a bounded session instead of inventing a full plan.

retrieved_evidence_expected.reason: today can tolerate a light touch

excellent_answer_traits: asks only one useful follow-up if needed
baseline_answer: Today should be easy with a small quality touch, such as strides, unless you have a near-term race target.
baseline_comparison_rubric: StrideIQ must use actual recent training
passing_answer: Today: easy run plus a small quality touch, like 6 x 20-second strides, because load is moderate and your last quality stimulus was 6 days ago. I would not invent a full plan from one question.
failing_answer: Start a 12-week generic base phase today.

situation.athlete_state: tempo workout scheduled, RPI tempo 6:35-6:45, recent threshold mile reps 6:25-6:30

conversation_turns.content: I have a tempo run today. What pace should I run?

user_message: I have a tempo run today. What pace should I run?

expected_coaching_truths.description: Use pace model but calibrate with recent workout evidence.

retrieved_evidence_expected.reason: pace model sets the starting range

baseline_answer: Start 6:40-ish and let it tighten only if effort stays controlled.
passing_answer: Run the first tempo segment at 6:35-6:45. Your recent mile reps were 6:25-6:30, so the model is not wildly high, but tempo should feel controlled rather than race pace.
failing_answer: Trust the zones over everything and run whatever feels comfortable.

situation.athlete_state: RPI says 5K pace 6:15, recent 16 x 400 at 5:35 pace

conversation_turns.content: The app says my 5K pace is 6:15 but I have been running 400s much faster. What should I use?

user_message: The app says my 5K pace is 6:15 but I have been running 400s much faster. What should I use?

expected_coaching_truths.description: Actual workout evidence can make zones stale.

excellent_answer_traits: uses the model as one input
baseline_answer: If the 400s were repeatable and recent, the 6:15 model pace is probably stale for short-race work.
baseline_comparison_rubric: StrideIQ should provide the practical pace implication
must_not: trust it over any other data
passing_answer: The 6:15 5K pace looks stale for this decision. I found the 16 x 400 session at about 5:35 pace, so use that as evidence that short-race pace can be faster than the model while still respecting race-day fade risk.
failing_answer: The app is always right. Ignore the workout and use 6:15.

situation.athlete_state: 8 x 800 planned, athlete has recently handled 90 second recoveries

conversation_turns.content: For 8 x 800 today, should I jog or walk the recoveries?

user_message: For 8 x 800 today, should I jog or walk the recoveries?

expected_coaching_truths.description: Recovery mode should serve the workout purpose.

baseline_answer: Use the recovery style that keeps the reps honest; walking is fine if quality is the point.
baseline_comparison_rubric: StrideIQ should give a clear rule

retrieved_evidence.content: 8 x 800 with 90 second walk recoveries completed

passing_answer: For the 8 x 800, walking is acceptable if the goal is fast, repeatable reps. You have handled 90 second recoveries before, so use that; jog only if the purpose is aerobic density rather than speed.
failing_answer: Always jog recoveries because walking recovery does not count.

situation.athlete_state: 1,100 calories logged by 2pm, evening run planned

conversation_turns.content: I logged 1,100 calories so far. Am I underfueling?

user_message: I logged 1,100 calories so far. Am I underfueling?

expected_coaching_truths.description: Do not treat partial-day logs as final totals.

baseline_answer: It is too low so far for a run day; add carbs and protein before the evening run and finish the day properly.
baseline_comparison_rubric: StrideIQ must not sum partial logs as final
StrideIQ should cite the run context
passing_answer: Yes, you are likely underfueled so far, but 1,100 is not your full day. Eat before the run: carbs now, some protein, then finish dinner instead of waiting until the day ends.
failing_answer: Your total today is 1,100 calories, so wait until the day ends before deciding.

situation.athlete_state: 5K race morning, leaving home 3 hours before race, considering half-dose Maurten bicarb

conversation_turns.content: I am leaving 3 hours before the race. Can I take half-dose Maurten bicarb?

user_message: I am leaving 3 hours before the race. Can I take half-dose Maurten bicarb?

expected_coaching_truths.description: Give a practical timing plan without pretending certainty.

baseline_answer: If you tolerate it and accept GI risk, half-dose before leaving is the practical option; do not make the race about the supplement.
passing_answer: If you use it, make it half-dose before leaving because the race is 3 hours away. The real tradeoff is GI risk, not fitness; if your stomach is uncertain, skip it and execute the 5K without making the supplement the story.
failing_answer: Definitely take it; it is guaranteed to buffer lactate.

situation.athlete_state: late dinner not logged, breakfast and lunch logged, long run tomorrow

conversation_turns.content: The app says I only had 1,600 calories yesterday, but I forgot to log dinner. Am I in trouble for tomorrow's long run?

user_message: The app says I only had 1,600 calories yesterday, but I forgot to log dinner. Am I in trouble for tomorrow's long run?

expected_coaching_truths.description: Missing data should reduce certainty while still guiding fueling.

baseline_answer: You're not in trouble if dinner happened; make tonight and tomorrow morning carb-forward and hydrate.
baseline_comparison_rubric: StrideIQ should give long-run fueling actions
must_not: skip the long run because of one log
passing_answer: Not automatically. You forgot to log dinner, so 1,600 is incomplete; protect tomorrow by making tonight and breakfast carb-forward, then start the long run fueled.
failing_answer: You only ate 1,600 and the deficit is confirmed, so skip the long run because of one log.

situation.athlete_state: slept 5.5 hours, chronic baseline 6-6.5, easy run scheduled

conversation_turns.content: I slept 5.5 hours but feel fine. Should I run?

user_message: I slept 5.5 hours but feel fine. Should I run?

expected_coaching_truths.description: Interpret sleep against this athlete's baseline.

baseline_answer: Run easy if it is truly easy; shorten if it feels flat. A 5.5-hour night is below your baseline but not automatically a red flag.
baseline_comparison_rubric: StrideIQ should tailor to today's workout
passing_answer: Yes, run if it stays easy. 5.5 is below your baseline, not automatically a 7 hours minimum problem; shorten the easy run if legs feel flat.
failing_answer: Below 7 hours means no run, regardless of your baseline.

situation.athlete_state: high work stress, normal sleep, quality workout planned

conversation_turns.content: Work is awful but my sleep is fine. Should I force the workout?

user_message: Work is awful but my sleep is fine. Should I force the workout?
required_context: stress can affect readiness even with sleep

expected_coaching_truths.description: Stress may modify execution but does not require blanket rest.

retrieved_evidence_expected.reason: quality session requires a decision rule

baseline_answer: Warm up and decide. If stress shows up in effort or HR, cap it and move the workout.
baseline_comparison_rubric: StrideIQ should connect stress to execution
must_not: tell me what happened at work
passing_answer: Do not force it blindly. Stress is high even though sleep is normal, so warm up and use the first rep as the checkpoint; if effort is off, cap the workout and move it.
failing_answer: What is making you stressed? Push through no matter what once you explain it.

situation.athlete_state: six nights around 6 hours, long run scheduled, no acute soreness

conversation_turns.content: My sleep has been around 6 hours all week. Is tomorrow's long run still okay?

user_message: My sleep has been around 6 hours all week. Is tomorrow's long run still okay?

expected_coaching_truths.description: Chronic low sleep should cap long-run ambition.

baseline_answer: The run can happen, but cap the ambition and keep it easy; shorten if effort drifts.
passing_answer: Tomorrow's long run can happen, but cap it. 6 hours all week is a chronic drag, so keep the long run easy and shorten if effort or HR drifts.
failing_answer: It is just one bad night; make it a progression.

situation.athlete_state: between plans, 35 mpw recent average, no active target loaded

conversation_turns.content: I'm between plans and don't know what to do this week.

user_message: I'm between plans and don't know what to do this week.

expected_coaching_truths.description: Maintain rhythm without inventing a full block.

retrieved_evidence_expected.reason: must not pretend there is a plan

baseline_answer: Hold normal frequency and one light quality touch while you choose the next anchor event.
baseline_comparison_rubric: StrideIQ should avoid a fake plan
passing_answer: Maintain the rhythm: about 35 mpw, normal frequency, and one quality touch. I would not start a new plan until we know the next anchor event.
failing_answer: Start a new 12-week race-specific block with five hard days.

situation.athlete_state: between plans, athlete asks for aggressive build, no race selected

conversation_turns.content: I want to build hard even though I do not have a race picked. Give me a plan.

user_message: I want to build hard even though I do not have a race picked. Give me a plan.

expected_coaching_truths.description: No anchor event means no aggressive specialization.

bad_coaching_patterns: hard build and a peak week
baseline_answer: Without a race, do not build hard; maintain, sharpen lightly, and choose the next target first.
baseline_comparison_rubric: StrideIQ should give a useful holding pattern

retrieved_evidence.content: no race selected no active plan

passing_answer: No race selected means no hard build yet. Use maintenance with one quality touch and keep volume steady; pick the anchor before we specialize.
failing_answer: Specialize now with a hard build and a peak week.

situation.athlete_state: race ended three days ago, athlete feels flat, next target unknown

conversation_turns.content: My race is done and I feel flat. What should the next week look like?

user_message: My race is done and I feel flat. What should the next week look like?

expected_coaching_truths.description: The week should absorb the race before rebuilding.

retrieved_evidence_expected.reason: next block should not be invented

baseline_answer: Take an absorption week: easy running, strides only if you bounce back, then choose the next target.
baseline_comparison_rubric: StrideIQ should give a bridge week
passing_answer: This is an absorption week. The race was three days ago, so keep everything easy, add strides only if you rebound, and do not start a new block until the next target is chosen.
failing_answer: Start intervals tomorrow and rebuild immediately.

situation.athlete_state: half marathon in 4 weeks, strong long runs, limited threshold work

conversation_turns.content: I have a half marathon in 4 weeks. Am I ready?

user_message: I have a half marathon in 4 weeks. Am I ready?

expected_coaching_truths.description: Readiness should name both built capacity and limiter.

baseline_answer: You are generally ready to finish well, but threshold durability is the limiter to sharpen over the next three weeks.

retrieved_evidence.content: long runs strong threshold limited half marathon in 4 weeks

passing_answer: You are ready to race the half, with one limiter: threshold durability. The block shows strong long runs, but threshold work is limited, so the next 4 weeks should sharpen sustained work without chasing volume.
failing_answer: You are definitely ready because your last long run was long.

situation.athlete_state: 10K in 3 weeks, speed strong, tempo absent, athlete wants more 400s

conversation_turns.content: My 10K is in 3 weeks. Should I keep doing 400s?

user_message: My 10K is in 3 weeks. Should I keep doing 400s?

expected_coaching_truths.description: Move from pure speed toward 10K-specific sustained work.

baseline_answer: Keep a small speed touch, but the main work should shift to threshold/10K-specific intervals.

retrieved_evidence.content: speed strong tempo absent 10K in 3 weeks

passing_answer: Not as the main workout. With the 10K in 3 weeks and speed already strong, keep a small 400s touch but make threshold or sustained 10K work the priority.
failing_answer: Do more 400s because speed is everything.

situation.athlete_state: missed last long run, marathon 5 weeks away, prior long-run base solid

conversation_turns.content: I missed my long run. Should I make it up midweek for the marathon?

user_message: I missed my long run. Should I make it up midweek for the marathon?

expected_coaching_truths.description: Do not cram a missed long run if the base exists.

retrieved_evidence_expected.reason: block context determines whether makeup is needed

baseline_answer: Do not cram it midweek; resume the plan and let the next long run be normal unless there were multiple missed weeks.
baseline_comparison_rubric: StrideIQ must know it is one miss
StrideIQ should preserve the next key day

retrieved_evidence.content: prior long-run base solid missed last long run marathon 5 weeks

passing_answer: Do not make it up midweek. You missed one long run, but the prior long-run base is solid; resume the marathon plan and protect the next key sessions instead of cramming.
failing_answer: Make it up midweek and double up long runs.

situation.athlete_state: 5K race morning, athlete wants 5:50 first mile, recent fast workout evidence

conversation_turns.content: I have a 5K this morning. I'm going out at 5:50 pace.

user_message: I have a 5K this morning. I'm going out at 5:50 pace.

expected_coaching_truths.description: Race-day answer should provide execution labels and not merely warn.

retrieved_evidence_expected.reason: packet supplies race and workout evidence

baseline_answer: Commit to the first mile, keep the second from leaking, and race the last mile by effort rather than fear.
baseline_comparison_rubric: StrideIQ must include private workout evidence
StrideIQ should outperform by tactical specificity
passing_answer: Timeline: get settled early. Warmup: finish with fast strides. Mile by mile: open near 5:50, accept a controlled positive split if it comes, and fight to keep mile 2 honest. Cue: commit, then compete.
failing_answer: That is aggressive relative to threshold. Negative split is always best, so do not race hard.

situation.athlete_state: same-day 5K, packet pickup, 90 minute drive, half-dose bicarb

conversation_turns.content: I have to leave 3 hours before the 5K. How do I time the bicarb and warmup?

user_message: I have to leave 3 hours before the 5K. How do I time the bicarb and warmup?

expected_coaching_truths.description: Integrate supplement, travel, pickup, and warmup.

bad_coaching_patterns: take it at the start line
baseline_answer: Take it before leaving if you accept the stomach risk, then preserve a real warmup after packet pickup.

retrieved_evidence.content: 3 hours before race 90 minute drive packet pickup

passing_answer: Take the half-dose before leaving if your stomach accepts the GI risk, because you have 3 hours, a 90 minute drive, and packet pickup. Preserve the warmup after pickup; do not let the supplement crowd out race prep.
failing_answer: Take it at the start line and skip warmup.

situation.athlete_state: race morning, weather unavailable, athlete asks clothing and pacing

conversation_turns.content: What should I wear and should I change my pace for the weather?

user_message: What should I wear and should I change my pace for the weather?

expected_coaching_truths.description: Do not fabricate weather while still giving decision rules.

baseline_answer: If weather data is unavailable, decide at the venue: dress for mile two, not the start line, and adjust pace only for heat/wind.
passing_answer: Weather unavailable, so do not change pace from invented data. Use a venue decision rule: layers for waiting, race kit for mile two, and only adjust pace if heat or wind is obvious.
failing_answer: It will be 55 degrees with calm wind, so pace normally.

situation.athlete_state: 16 mile long run, last 4 miles HR drifted and pace faded

conversation_turns.content: I just did my long run. How did it go?

user_message: I just did my long run. How did it go?

expected_coaching_truths.description: Interpret the run's training meaning.

baseline_answer: The long run was useful but showed late durability/fueling fade; recover before the next quality session.

retrieved_evidence.content: HR drift last 4 miles pace faded

passing_answer: This was productive but not perfectly steady: HR drift showed up in the last 4 miles as pace faded. That points to fatigue or fuel durability, so recover before the next quality session.
failing_answer: Great effort, keep it up. Perfect execution with no fade.

situation.athlete_state: failed 1200 session last week, completed 4 x mile this week

conversation_turns.content: I bounced back after failing the 1200s. What does today's 4 x mile mean?

user_message: I bounced back after failing the 1200s. What does today's 4 x mile mean?

expected_coaching_truths.description: Read the workout as evidence of rebound, not isolated success.

baseline_answer: The 4 x mile shows you recovered from the failed 1200 day and have better sustained control than that one bad session implied.

retrieved_evidence.content: failed 1200 session previous week 4 x mile completed this week

passing_answer: This is a real rebound. The failed 1200 session did not define your fitness; completing 4 x mile shows sustained durability came back.
failing_answer: Ignore last week. A single workout proves everything.

situation.athlete_state: run has pace and splits but HR missing

conversation_turns.content: My HR did not record. Can you still tell me how the run went?

user_message: My HR did not record. Can you still tell me how the run went?

expected_coaching_truths.description: Use available data and state HR limitation.

baseline_answer: Yes, but confidence is lower. Use pace/splits for execution and avoid claims about aerobic drift.
passing_answer: Yes, but HR is missing, so I will not claim cardiac drift. Using pace and splits, the run looks steady; confidence is lower without HR.
failing_answer: Your heart rate was high and cardiac drift is confirmed.

situation.athlete_state: coach gave generic negative-split 5K advice, athlete corrects with record-race reality

conversation_turns.content: How should I pace the 5K?
Aim for a negative split.
That's not how 5Ks are raced at the sharp end.

user_message: That's not how 5Ks are raced at the sharp end.

expected_coaching_truths.description: Repair the actual coaching model, not just apologize.

baseline_answer: Fair correction: for an aggressive 5K, controlled positive splitting can be the right tactical frame; the key is not panicking in mile two.
baseline_comparison_rubric: StrideIQ should turn correction into better plan
passing_answer: Fair correction: for an aggressive 5K, a controlled positive split can be the right model. The plan is 5K-specific: commit early, keep mile two from leaking, then fight the fade.
failing_answer: I apologize. Negative split is still always best, so use the same advice.

situation.athlete_state: coach failed to find March 28 16 x 400, athlete gives date

conversation_turns.content: I did 16 x 400 faster than that.
I cannot see that workout.
It was March 28. I just went directly to it.

user_message: It was March 28. I just went directly to it.

expected_coaching_truths.description: Use the correction as retrieval key and revise answer.

baseline_answer: Found it. That workout changes the pace evidence, so the prior answer should be revised.
baseline_comparison_rubric: StrideIQ must repair trust with evidence
passing_answer: Found March 28: 16 x 400. Using that, the prior pace read was too conservative; that session is evidence you can handle faster 5K-specific rhythm.
failing_answer: I still cannot access it, so maybe you mean another activity.

situation.athlete_state: athlete gives wrong date, nearby activity has matching title

conversation_turns.content: Find my 8 x 800 from April 2.
I do not see it.
Check the week around then.

user_message: Check the week around then.

expected_coaching_truths.description: Search around the date and label uncertainty.

retrieved_evidence_expected.reason: fallback search should find likely match

baseline_answer: I found a likely nearby 8 x 800; confirm whether that is the one before using it as evidence.
baseline_comparison_rubric: StrideIQ must not call athlete wrong

retrieved_evidence.content: week around nearby 8 x 800 likely match

passing_answer: Searching the week around then found a likely 8 x 800 nearby. I would use it only after you confirm it is the one, because the date may be off.
failing_answer: No match, done. You are wrong.

situation.athlete_state: athlete feels slower, recent workouts show improvement under fatigue

conversation_turns.content: I feel like I'm getting slower.

user_message: I feel like I'm getting slower.

expected_coaching_truths.description: Validate emotion but answer with data.

bad_coaching_patterns: it is normal to feel that way
baseline_answer: The feeling is real, but the data does not show a slowdown; it shows fatigue sitting on top of improving workouts.
passing_answer: The feeling is real, but the data does not show you are getting slower. Recent workouts improved under fatigue, so this looks like load masking fitness, not regression.
failing_answer: It is normal to feel that way. Do not worry; everything is fine.

situation.athlete_state: high stress, wants to run for relief, easy day available

conversation_turns.content: I'm stressed and just want to run.

user_message: I'm stressed and just want to run.

expected_coaching_truths.description: Allow running as regulation when safe and bounded.
must_include_any: do not turn it into a workout

bad_coaching_patterns: what is going on in your life
baseline_answer: Run easy for relief, but cap it and do not turn stress into a workout.
passing_answer: Run, but keep it easy. You are stressed and today can be an easy day, so use 20-40 minutes as regulation and do not turn it into a workout.
failing_answer: What is going on in your life? Skip running because stress.

situation.athlete_state: athlete PR'd but feels disappointed by fade

conversation_turns.content: I PR'd but I'm disappointed because I faded badly.

user_message: I PR'd but I'm disappointed because I faded badly.

expected_coaching_truths.description: Hold the PR and the fade as true.

baseline_answer: Both things can be true: the PR counts, and the fade gives the next execution lesson.
baseline_comparison_rubric: StrideIQ should convert disappointment into next step
passing_answer: Both are true: the PR counts, and the late fade is the lesson. Do not erase the PR; use the fade to sharpen pacing and durability for the next race.
failing_answer: Only celebrate. You should not be disappointed.

situation.athlete_state: knee pain after downhill long run, no swelling, easy run scheduled

conversation_turns.content: My knee hurts after yesterday's run. Should I run today?

user_message: My knee hurts after yesterday's run. Should I run today?

expected_coaching_truths.description: Give a modify/skip rule based on pain signals.

baseline_answer: Do not force it. If walking is pain-free, try 10 minutes easy and stop if pain changes your stride; otherwise skip.
passing_answer: Do not run through knee pain. If walking is pain-free, use a 10-minute easy test and stop if pain changes your stride; otherwise skip today's easy run.
failing_answer: Run through it, or see a doctor immediately for any knee pain.

situation.athlete_state: Achilles stiff in morning after speed session, workout scheduled tomorrow

conversation_turns.content: My Achilles is stiff this morning after speed work. Can I run?

user_message: My Achilles is stiff this morning after speed work. Can I run?

expected_coaching_truths.description: Morning Achilles stiffness after speed is a warning signal.

baseline_answer: No speed today. If it loosens completely with walking, easy only; if not, skip and protect the tendon.
passing_answer: Achilles morning stiffness after speed work is a caution signal. No speed today; if it fully loosens with walking, easy only, otherwise skip and protect tomorrow.
failing_answer: Ignore morning stiffness, stretch aggressively, and do hill sprints.

situation.athlete_state: general quad soreness after lifting, no sharp pain, easy run scheduled

conversation_turns.content: My quads are sore from lifting. Is this an injury reason to skip?

user_message: My quads are sore from lifting. Is this an injury reason to skip?

expected_coaching_truths.description: Differentiate normal soreness from warning pain.

retrieved_evidence_expected.reason: easy run can be used as flush

baseline_answer: Normal lifting soreness is not automatically an injury reason; run easy if gait is normal and stop for sharp pain.
passing_answer: Sore quads from lifting are not automatically an injury. Run easy if your gait is normal, and skip if the soreness turns sharp or changes mechanics.
failing_answer: All soreness is injury, but run hard to flush it.
